items.q: Why is GDP per capita a poor measure of wellbeing?
items.correct: GDP measures economic activity, not wellbeing. It ignores inequality, unpaid work, environmental damage and quality of life — high GDP can coexist with poor health, education or happiness.
items.misconception: A high GDP per capita means citizens are happy and well off.
items.random_wrong: GDP measures the total wealth of every individual in a country.
items.why: Equatorial Guinea has high GDP per capita but very low HDI — the wealth is concentrated. GDP is an average, blind to distribution and non-market activities (parenting, environmental services).

items.q: Why is 'renewable' not the same as 'clean' energy?
items.correct: Renewable means the source replenishes naturally on human timescales; clean means low environmental/health impact. Hydropower is renewable but floods habitats; bioenergy is renewable but can drive deforestation.
items.misconception: All renewable energy sources are clean and have no environmental impact.
items.random_wrong: Clean energy means it doesn't produce any smoke when burned.
items.why: Large dams cause major ecological and social harm despite being renewable. Nuclear is low-carbon but not renewable. The two categories overlap but don't match — important for energy-policy evaluation.

items.q: Why is a food web a better model than a food chain?
items.correct: A food chain shows one linear path; a food web shows the many interconnected feeding relationships in reality, so it better represents how species depend on multiple food sources.
items.misconception: A food web is the same as a food chain, just drawn differently.
items.random_wrong: A food web shows what spiders eat and a food chain shows what other animals eat.
items.why: Most species have multiple predators and prey. A linear chain is a simplification — the web captures redundancy and resilience. Removing one species rarely collapses a real ecosystem.

items.q: Which current keeps the UK mild?
items.why: Correct answer: Gulf Stream. “Pacific Drift” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which part of the UK is wettest?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: West. “East” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: From which direction does the UK's prevailing wind come (compass degrees, south-west)?
items.why: Exact answer: 225. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
